retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Joint Sound
retrieved_docs.content: The two common joint sounds are clicking and crepitations. Clicking is a sound of an uncoordinated movement of the condylar head and the articular disc. Crepitations are compound sounds caused by the roughened, irregular articular surfaces during mandibular movement.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Temporomandibular disorder classic triad includes pain in the temporomandibular joint, reduced mandibular range of motion, and functional clicking. This can impair patients' capacity to perform everyday tasks such as eating, speaking, or yawning, significantly impacting their quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Recommended Systematic and Psychological Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Temporomandibular disorder symptoms include pain, TMJ clicking and crepitation, and different levels of mandibular limitation. The pain is typically provoked by function; spontaneous pain in the TMJ area suggests a different etiology. [8] Pain can refer to the neck and scalp and tends to be exacerbated by masticating, yawning, or talking for long periods. [1] A click, crepitus, or pop when opening or closing the mouth may be associated with anterior disc displacement or osteoarthritis. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Otalgia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Possible Reasons for Secondary Otalgia
retrieved_docs.content: TMJ condition: TMJ dysfunction, bruxism

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Pain on mandibular movement, headaches, and referred pain point towards a muscular cause; tenderness on TMJ palpation and joint sounds suggest an intraarticular problem. [1] Evaluating the oral cavity, the inner ear (otoscopy), and palpating the neck musculature help exclude other causes of facial pain. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Otologic Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: TMJ pain in the auricular regions is more noticeable posteriorly. Tinnitus, itching in the ear, and vertigo are other symptoms associated with articular pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Disc Dislocation with Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Disc displacement can lead to partial or complete disarticulation of the disc from discal space in condyle–disc assembly. When the mouth is closed, the articular disc is displaced anterior to the condyle head; when the mouth opens, the disc repositions on the condyle head similarly to normal. [6] This on and off disc movement explains the click, snap, or pop sound in the TMJ. This sound does not appear with every movement of the mandible but with some frequency. [6] A normal range of motion is expected since the articular disc reduces during condylar translation. [6] Jaw deviation while opening the mouth can occur; the interincisal distance of disc reduction during opening is greater than when the disc is dislocated during the closure. The disc can sometimes fail to reduce with consequent mouth opening limitation. This is known as disc displacement with reduction with intermittent locking. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Temporomandibular disorder (TMD) refers to a group of conditions involving the orofacial region divided into those affecting the masticatory muscles and those affecting the temporomandibular joint (TMJ). The typical features include pain in TMJ, restriction of mandibular movement, and TMJ sounds.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be referred to a maxillofacial surgeon in cases of severe pain and dysfunction, ineffective conservative treatment, and a history of trauma of the TMJ. Lomas et. al. [1] summarized the following “red flag” symptoms that require specialist referral: Persistent and worsening pain Trismus Cranial nerve abnormalities Neurologic dysfunction Concurrent infection Systemic illness Weight loss Asymmetrical neck or facial swelling Unilateral hearing loss Vestibular dysfunction New onset or unilateral tinnitus

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Excessive driving represents a potential cause of impaired TMJ function. Longer driving durations increase the risk of developing temporomandibular disorders, although the underlying mechanisms remain unclear. [27] Neck pain and shoulder pain also constitute possible risk factors for joint dysfunction. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: No initial laboratory testing is necessary for isolated TMJ dislocation. Women of childbearing age should have a pregnancy test performed before performing imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Temporomandibular disorder (TMD) refers to a group of conditions involving the orofacial region divided into those affecting the masticatory muscles and those affecting the temporomandibular joint (TMJ). The typical features include pain in TMJ, restriction of mandibular movement, and TMJ sounds. These symptoms may resolve by themselves without further treatment. If not, conservative methods are the first to be used with positive results in most patients. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of temporomandibular disorder and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Dystonia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Oro-mandibular dystonia can manifest as night-time bruxism (jaw spasms producing forceful jaw closure) and may follow dental procedures, oro-mandibular-facial trauma, or temporomandibular joint dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Subluxation
retrieved_docs.content: It is a non-pathologic condition, a repeatable clinical phenomenon characterized by a sudden forward movement of the condyle past the crest of the articular eminence during the final stages of mouth opening. The steep, short posterior slope of the articular eminence and the longer anterior slope - more superior to the crest - cause the condyle to subluxate. The examiner can witness it by requesting the patient to open wide, creating a small void or depression behind the condyle.

retrieved_docs.title: Eagle Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, the syndrome is most frequently seen in the third or fourth decades. Although there is no significant sex predilection in the occurrence of mineralization of the styloid process, symptoms are more common in women. In Eagle syndrome, the symptoms range from mild discomfort to acute neurologic and referred pain. Symptoms are divided into two groups. The first group of symptoms, also called the “classic Eagle syndrome,” is usually encountered in patients after pharyngeal trauma or tonsillectomy.  It is characterized by pain located in the areas where the fifth, seventh, eighth, ninth, and tenth cranial nerves are distributed. As mentioned earlier, pain is presumably a consequence of stretching or compression of the cranial nerves V, VII, VIII, IX, or X  or their nerve endings in the tonsillar fossa during healing (scar tissue). Although an elongated styloid process may cause episodic tic-like pain attacks that are typical of glossopharyngeal neuralgia, most patients present with a constant dull pharyngeal pain, focused in the ipsilateral tonsillar fossa, that can be referred to the ear and aggravated by rotation of the head. Other symptoms include the sensation of a foreign body in the pharynx (55%), difficulty swallowing(dysphagia), painful swallowing, otalgia, headache, pain along with the distribution of the external and internal carotid arteries, pain on cervical rotation or mastication, facial pain, and tinnitus. Further, a mass or bulge may be palpated in the ipsilateral tonsillar fossa, exacerbating the patient’s symptoms. Classic Eagle syndrome usually presents on only one side. However, it may rarely have a bilateral presentation. The other group of symptoms is attributed to vascular Eagle syndrome, or stylocarotid syndrome, which is a consequence of compression of the internal or external carotid artery (along with their perivascular sympathetic fibers) by a laterally or medially deviated styloid process. In these cases, turning the head can cause compression of the artery, which causes pain along with the distribution of the artery.  This can potentially lead to TIAs, vertigo, and syncope. Usually, there is no history of tonsillectomy. In the case of impingement of the internal carotid artery, pain is often referred to the supraorbital region. In the case of external carotid artery irritation, the pain radiates to the infraorbital region.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The TMJ performs complex movements across multiple orthogonal planes and axes of rotation, functioning synergistically with the other components (see Image . Temporomandibular Joint). Coordination with the contralateral TMJ ensures synchronized movement. Dysfunction or pathology of this structure can lead to pain, limited jaw movement, and impaired quality of life. Medical management focuses on diagnosing disorders such as TMJ dysfunction, arthritis, and trauma. Surgical intervention may be necessary for severe joint degeneration, ankylosis, tumors, or congenital anomalies.

retrieved_docs.content: these interconnections demonstrate how peripheral‑nerve pathology, entrapment‑syndrome decompression, pain and migraine mechanisms, and musculoskeletal disorders converge in neurological and musculoskeletal patient care, guiding diagnosis, therapeutic planning, and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Head injury trauma is frequently observed in the context of a حادث سير (traffic accident). In addition, a حادث سير commonly co‑occurs with وجع في الكتف (shoulder pain). Consequently, head injury trauma, traffic accidents, and shoulder pain are interrelated clinical findings that often appear together in patients involved in vehicular collisions, underscoring the importance of evaluating both cranial and musculoskeletal injuries after such events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx are classified as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, indicating that these examinations are considered part of the broader group of nasal operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine and Headache Syndromes are categorized as a subcategory of the broader condition known as اعتلال عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular disease). This hierarchical relationship suggests that migraine and headache disorders may be viewed within the context of neuromuscular pathology, highlighting the importance of considering underlying neuromuscular factors when diagnosing and managing patients presenting with these headache syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 438
retrieved_docs.content: ureteral obstruction, seizures, lung disorders, bone and joint inflammation, female infertility, endometrial implantation, tongue disorders, mitral valve stenosis, retinal detachment, paralysis, lower‑extremity ulcers and headache including migraine, indicating a birth‑related component across these entities. Osteoarthritis is categorized as a form of bone and joint inflammation (التهاب العظم والمفصل), and female infertility is associated with endometrial implantation, reflecting the known link between infertility and endometriosis. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how therapeutic strategies, preventive measures, developmental biology, microbial factors and congenital status intersect across diverse medical conditions, providing an integrated framework for understanding disease mechanisms and guiding comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Headache; including migraine is classified as a condition that belongs to the broader category of Nervous system and sense organ diseases. The category Nervous system and sense organ diseases also includes the child entity اضطراب العين (eye disorder). Recognizing that both headache—including migraine—and eye disorders are subtypes of Nervous system and sense organ diseases helps clinicians organize related symptoms, consider shared pathophysiological mechanisms, and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1781

This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2200
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic mixed headache syndrome is recognized as a chronic nervous system disorder, indicating that this specific headache condition falls within the broader category of chronic disorders affecting the nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2199
retrieved_docs.content: System disorder of the nervous system is a broad classification that encompasses conditions such as صداع وعائي (vascular headache), indicating that vascular headache is considered a specific manifestation within the wider category of nervous system disorders.
